title: Quick check: review and revoke OAuth grants

questions.prompt: Why is OAuth-grant review a separate playbook step from session revoke and password reset?
choices: OAuth tokens are persistent and independent of the password and interactive sessions.

choices.label: Because OAuth grants take 24 hours to expire on their own.
choices.rationale: They do not expire automatically in a timeframe useful for response.

choices.label: Because Huntress charges per step.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Because OAuth revoke is faster than the earlier steps.
choices.rationale: Speed is not the reason; persistence-mechanism distinctness is.

questions.explanation: Skipping this step leaves an attacker-controlled app authenticated while the user looks secure. OAuth tokens survive session revoke and password reset. The attacker's app keeps authenticating independently until the grant is explicitly revoked.

questions.prompt: The targeted-revoke discipline says you revoke:
choices: The grants from the compromise window, not all grants.

choices.label: All grants the user has, to be safe.
choices.rationale: Blanket revoke disrupts legitimate integrations.

choices.label: Only the grants the user identifies as unfamiliar.
choices.rationale: User recollection is not the discriminator; the compromise window is.

choices.label: All grants with broad scopes regardless of timing.
choices.rationale: Broad scopes on legitimate verified-publisher apps are common.

questions.explanation: Long-standing legitimate integrations stay intact. Compromise-window targeting beats blanket revoke. Grants from before the window are legitimate-by-context.

questions.prompt: Step 4 on Kira. Recommendation: revoke MailSync-Free (granted 11 minutes after suspicious sign-in). Grants list: (a) MailSync-Free (malicious); (b) Trello (6 months old, verified publisher, Calendars.Read); (c) OneDriveDirector (3 days old, unverified publisher, Files.ReadWrite). What do you do?

choices.label: Revoke all three to be thorough.
choices.rationale: Trello is verified-publisher and long-standing. Disrupts the user's workflow.

choices.label: Revoke only MailSync-Free and ignore the others.
choices.rationale: OneDriveDirector within the compromise window with broad scopes is worth surfacing.

questions.explanation: Reply to the SOC about OneDriveDirector (3 days old, unverified publisher, broad scope, in compromise window). Leave Trello alone. Targeted revoke per the Recommendation. Ambiguous-in-window finding surfaces to the SOC. Legitimate long-standing grant stays.

questions.prompt: Two hours after revoking OneDriveDirector (with SOC sign-off), the Timeline shows a new OAuth grant for the same app, 30 minutes after your revoke. What do you do?

choices.label: Re-revoke and close.
choices.rationale: A new grant event means someone re-authorised it. Whack-a-mole revocation hides the cause.

choices: Check whether the user re-granted in confusion or whether the attacker has another path.

choices.label: The revoke did not work; re-revoke.
choices.rationale: The first revoke worked. A new grant event is a new authorisation, not a failed revoke.

questions.explanation: Bump to senior given the recurring pattern. Re-grant post-revoke is a signal. Either user confusion or incomplete remediation. Investigate the pattern rather than re-revoking in a loop.

questions.prompt: "Revoking all OAuth grants to clean up" has what consequence?
choices: Disrupts legitimate integrations the user actively relies on.

choices.label: Nothing. Blanket revoke is the safe default.
choices.rationale: The lesson is explicit about both costs.

choices.label: It crashes the identity provider.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: It triggers an automatic tenant-wide alert.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Trains them to be paranoid about granting any access in the future. Blanket revoke breaks working integrations and erodes user trust in OAuth consent. The targeted discipline preserves legitimate grants.

questions.prompt: The suspicious grant is admin-consented at the tenant level rather than per-user. What does the lesson say?
choices: Tenant-level grants affect every user. Revoking them is senior territory. Bump.

choices.label: Revoke as normal at the user level.
choices.rationale: User-level revoke cannot reach tenant-consented grants.

choices.label: Ignore tenant-level grants because they are outside the user surface.
choices.rationale: They are a tenant-wide compromise indicator. Bump, do not ignore.

choices.label: Wait for the SOC to update the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Senior is the escalation surface for tenant-level mechanisms.

questions.explanation: User-level revoke does not touch tenant-consented grants. Senior territory.

questions.prompt: A grant has scopes like Mail.ReadWrite.All or Directory.ReadWrite.All. What does the lesson say?

choices.label: Revoke as normal. Broad scopes are common.
choices.rationale: .All scopes specifically cross the tenant ceiling.

choices: Those scopes extend beyond the affected user to the tenant. Bump.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket for guidance.
choices.rationale: Senior is the escalation path.

choices.label: Ignore. Scopes are not part of the playbook.
choices.rationale: Scopes are part of the grant review.

questions.explanation: .All scopes are tenant-wide. A grant with Directory.ReadWrite.All during a compromise window is above the helpdesk ceiling.

questions.prompt: It is safe to skip verification on OAuth revokes because the portal always processes them synchronously.
questions.explanation: Some identity providers process OAuth revokes asynchronously. The portal may show the revoke as queued but not yet effective. Check that the grant disappears from the user's app list.

questions.prompt: The user has many grants from the compromise window (more than two or three). What does the lesson say?

choices.label: Revoke all of them and close.
choices.rationale: Without senior review the multi-grant pattern is missed.

choices.label: Pick the most suspicious one and revoke that only.
choices.rationale: The pattern (many grants in window) is the signal, not one specific grant.

choices.label: Treat it as normal SaaS sprawl.
choices.rationale: Many grants concentrated in the compromise window is not normal sprawl.

questions.explanation: Could indicate the attacker was granting multiple apps to maximise persistence; The response scope may need to widen. Multi-grant pattern in the compromise window is itself a signal worth escalating. The attacker may be establishing redundant persistence.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a named attribute you review on an OAuth grant?

choices.label: App display name.
choices.rationale: Named in the lesson.

choices.label: Publisher verification status.
choices.rationale: Named in the lesson.

choices: The exact bytes of the OAuth refresh token.

choices.label: Scopes (Mail.Read, Files.ReadWrite, etc.).
choices.rationale: Named in the lesson.

questions.explanation: The refresh-token bytes are not visible to the tech and are not part of the review. You review app name, publisher, scopes, granted timestamp, and last-used timestamp.
